docs.title: Skill: MS365 Automation
docs.text: Create Power Automate workflows and automate repetitive tasks in MS365 ecosystem. Reduces manual work through automation in Outlook, SharePoint, Excel, Teams, etc.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Attribute Value Skill ID skill-ms365-automation Type execution (procedural workflow) Version 1.0 Status ACTIVE Created 2026-04-08 Last Reviewed 2026-04-08 Dependencies skill-prompting-core Domains professional-work

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Automate repetitive MS365 tasks.

Designs and creates Power Automate flows to eliminate manual, repetitive work. Uses Outlook, SharePoint, Excel, Teams, Power BI, and other MS365 tools to build automated workflows, reduce errors, and improve efficiency.

docs.title: When to Use This Skill
docs.text:
• Use this skill when: You have a repetitive MS365 task that could be automated
  • Do NOT use this skill when: The task is one-time or manual judgment is better than automation
  • Prerequisite state: Clear description of the task to automate; you have access to required MS365 tools

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
• Task to automate: Clear description of what you want automated
  • Current process: How is this done manually now?
  • Trigger: What starts the workflow?

docs.title: Optional Inputs
docs.text:
• Frequency: How often does this happen?
  • Data sources: What systems/data are involved?
  • Success criteria: How do you know it worked?
  • Error handling: What should happen if something goes wrong?

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
• Automation is possible in MS365 (not everything can be automated)
  • You have access to required tools and data sources
  • The process is stable enough to automate
  • You'll test and refine the automation before fully deploying

docs.title: Phase 1: Assess Automation Feasibility
docs.text:
Goal: Determine if this task can be automated and is worth automating.

  1. Analyze current process:
  2. What steps happen? (in order)
  3. How much time does it take?
  4. How often does it happen?

  5. What data is involved?

  6. Assess automation potential:

  7. Is this repetitive and consistent?
  8. Can it be triggered automatically?
  9. Are the data sources accessible in MS365?

  10. Would automation save meaningful time?

  11. Identify blockers:

  12. Are there steps that require human judgment?
  13. Are there external systems not accessible to Power Automate?
  14. Would partial automation be useful?

Output: Feasibility assessment

Approval Gate: SOFT ("Is automation worth it for this?") - If YES: Continue to design - If NO/PARTIAL: Discuss alternatives or simplified version

docs.title: Phase 2: Design the Workflow
docs.text:
Goal: Plan the automation flow before building.

  1. Map the workflow:
  2. Trigger: "When [event] happens..."
  3. Steps: "Then [action], then [action]..."
  4. Decision points: "If [condition], then [path A], else [path B]"

  5. Outputs: "Result: [what happens]"

  6. Identify data flows:

  7. What data comes in?
  8. What transformations happen?
  9. What data goes where?

  10. Error handling: what if data is missing/wrong?

  11. Plan error scenarios:

  12. What if the trigger fails?
  13. What if a step fails?
  14. How will errors be handled/reported?
  15. Should there be a fallback/manual review?

Output: Detailed workflow design

Approval Gate: YES ("Does this workflow look right?") - If NO: Refine design - If YES: Proceed to building

docs.title: Phase 3: Build the Power Automate Flow
docs.text:
Goal: Implement the automation.

  1. Create Power Automate flow:
  2. Set trigger: "When [event] happens"
  3. Add actions: use Power Automate connectors
  4. Add conditions: if/then logic

  5. Add error handling: error notification or alternative path

  6. Configure connectors:

  7. Outlook (email, calendar)
  8. SharePoint (lists, documents)
  9. Excel (workbooks, tables)
  10. Power BI (data)
  11. Teams (messages, channels)

  12. Custom connectors if needed

  13. Test flow:

  14. Run manually with test data
  15. Check each step executes correctly
  16. Verify outputs are correct
  17. Test error scenarios

Output: Working Power Automate flow

Approval Gate: SOFT (testing complete, ready to deploy)

docs.title: Phase 4: Refine and Optimize
docs.text:
Goal: Make the workflow efficient and robust.

  1. Performance optimization:
  2. Are there unnecessary steps?
  3. Can parallel processes speed it up?

  4. Can filtering reduce data processed?

  5. Error handling:

  6. What errors are logged?
  7. How are errors communicated?

  8. Is manual review needed for errors?

  9. Monitoring:

  10. How will you know if the flow is working?
  11. What metrics matter? (speed, success rate, errors)

  12. How often should you review?

  13. Documentation:

  14. What does this flow do?
  15. When was it created/last modified?
  16. Who maintains it?
  17. What could break it?

Output: Optimized, documented flow

Approval Gate: SOFT (ready for production)

docs.title: Phase 5: Deploy and Monitor
docs.text:
Goal: Put the automation into production and track it.

  1. Enable the flow:
  2. Turn on automated trigger
  3. Begin running on schedule

  4. Monitor first runs

  5. Track performance:

  6. Success rate (how often does it complete successfully?)
  7. Failure rate (when does it fail and why?)
  8. Execution time (how long does it take?)

  9. Manual interventions (how often does it need help?)

  10. Alert on problems:

  11. Set up notifications for failures
  12. Review failed runs

  13. Fix issues quickly

  14. Refine based on real usage:

  15. Did it save the expected time?
  16. Are there edge cases not handled?
  17. Can it be further optimized?

Output: Live, monitored automation

Approval Gate: None (monitoring is ongoing)

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
Workflow Documentation:

POWER AUTOMATE WORKFLOW: [Name]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PURPOSE
[What this automation does and why]

TRIGGER
When: [Event that starts the flow]
Frequency: [How often it runs]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

WORKFLOW STEPS

Step 1: [Action name]
├─ What it does: [Description]
└─ Configuration: [Relevant settings]

Step 2: [Action name]
├─ Condition: [If/Then logic, if applicable]
├─ What it does: [Description]
└─ Configuration: [Settings]

[Continue for all steps...]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ERROR HANDLING

If [error type]:
├─ Action: [What happens]
└─ Notification: [Who gets notified, how]

If [error type]:
├─ Action: [What happens]
└─ Retry: [Yes/No + approach]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

OUTPUTS & RESULTS

The flow produces:
├─ [Output 1]: [Where it goes, who sees it]
├─ [Output 2]: [Where it goes, who sees it]
└─ [Output 3]: [Where it goes, who sees it]

Success criteria:
├─ Criterion 1: [What indicates success]
└─ Criterion 2: [What indicates success]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

MONITORING & MAINTENANCE

What to watch:
├─ Success rate: [Target %, current %]
├─ Execution time: [Target, current]
└─ Failures: [When/why to expect them]

Review frequency: [How often to check]
Owners: [Who maintains this flow]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

docs.title: Style Rules
docs.text:
• Clear trigger: Workflow should activate on a specific, consistent event
  • Linear when possible: Prefer simple step-by-step over complex branching
  • Error first: Think about what could go wrong and handle it
  • Idempotent: Workflow should be safe to run multiple times (shouldn't duplicate)
  • Logged and monitored: Failures should be visible
  • Documented: Future you will need to understand this

docs.title: Pattern 1: Email Categorization & Filing
docs.text:
When: Email arrives with [specific conditions]
Then: Append to Excel table, create SharePoint item, move to folder

docs.title: Pattern 2: Approval Workflow
docs.text:
When: SharePoint item created
Then: Send email approval request to [person]
If: Approved, update item and notify creator
If: Rejected, return item and request changes

docs.title: Pattern 3: Data Sync
docs.text:
When: Excel table updated
Then: Update corresponding SharePoint list / Power BI
If: Error, notify owner and log

docs.title: Pattern 4: Scheduled Report
docs.text:
When: [Schedule, e.g., Monday 9am]
Then: Gather data from sources
Format as report
Email to distribution list

docs.title: Pattern 5: Task Creation
docs.text:
When: Email from specific sender OR email with [keywords]
Then: Create task in Outlook / To-Do
Assign to [person]
Set due date based on email

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Condition Resolution Flow fails to trigger Check trigger conditions. Verify event is actually happening. Review permissions. Step fails (connector error) Check permissions on target system (SharePoint, Excel, etc.). Verify data format. Test manually. Flow creates duplicates Add deduplication logic. Check for multiple triggers of same event. Use idempotency keys. Flow runs but produces wrong output Verify data transformations. Check formulas/expressions. Test with sample data. Flow is too slow Identify bottleneck step. Consider parallelization. Remove unnecessary steps. Optimize data filters.

docs.title: Related Skills
docs.text: Skill How Used skill-prompting-core Establish professional-work context [future: data-transformation] Complex data formatting and manipulation [future: integration-patterns] Deep integrations across systems

docs.title: Related Docs
docs.text:
• GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md — Professional-work constraints and standards
  • domains/professional-work-context.md — MS365 tools and automation autonomy
  • Power Automate documentation (Microsoft) — Connector reference and examples

docs.title: Version
docs.text:
• Created: 2026-04-08
  • Last reviewed: 2026-04-08
  • Next review: 2026-07-08 (quarterly)
  • Status: ACTIVE
  • Scope: Power Automate workflows and MS365 automation

docs.title: Skill: Prompting Core
docs.text: Foundational workflow. Every execution skill depends on this. Establishes the operating context before any downstream skill runs.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Attribute Value Skill ID skill-prompting-core Type execution (foundational) Version 1.0 Status ACTIVE Created 2026-04-08 Last Reviewed 2026-04-08 Dependencies None (foundational) Domains All

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Establish operating context before any downstream skill executes.

This skill reads three inputs (global context, domain context, repo context) and assembles them into a complete operating picture. All other execution skills call this first. No other skill should run until this skill has returned.

docs.title: When to Use This Skill
docs.text:
• Use this skill when: Starting any downstream skill, beginning a new task, context appears unclear or outdated
  • Do NOT use this skill when: You already have confirmed context and have explicitly read all three input files in this session
  • Prerequisite state: The user has provided a task description or identified a specific work stream

docs.title: Input Requirements
docs.text: This skill does not take direct inputs. Instead, it reads three context sources in a fixed order.

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text: • User task or question: What does the user want to accomplish?

docs.title: Files Read (in order)
docs.text:
1. GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md (repo root) — Universal operating principles
  2. Domain context file — Domain-specific rules and autonomy (inferred from task; if ambiguous, ask user)
  3. Repo context file (CLAUDE.md) — Repo-specific rules and pre-authorized operations

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
• All three context files exist and are current.
  • The user has read GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md at least once.
  • Repo-level rules in CLAUDE.md do not contradict global hard rules.

docs.title: Phase 1: Read Global Context
docs.text:
Goal: Load universal operating principles.

  1. Read GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md from repo root
  2. Extract key sections:
  3. Identity (name, roles, communication style)
  4. Hard Rules (five non-negotiable rules)
  5. Tools & Systems (approved tools and critical systems)
  6. Decision Framework (precedence hierarchy)
  7. Risk Tolerance (production vs. fail-fast contexts)
  8. Communication Preferences (directness, conciseness, assumptions)

Output: Global context summary

Approval Gate: None (foundational read, no decisions)

docs.title: Phase 2: Infer and Confirm Domain
docs.text:
Goal: Identify the domain for this task.

  1. Infer domain from user's task description (business-ventures, professional-work, parenting, etc.)
  2. If domain is obvious from context, proceed
  3. If domain is ambiguous, ask user: "This sounds like it could be [domain-a] or [domain-b]. Which applies?"
  4. Confirm domain before proceeding

Output: Domain confirmed

Approval Gate: Yes, if domain is ambiguous or unclear. User confirms which domain applies.

docs.title: Phase 3: Read Domain Context
docs.text:
Goal: Load domain-specific rules and autonomy calibration.

  1. Read domains/[domain]-context.md
  2. Extract key sections:
  3. Domain definition (what constitutes work in this domain)
  4. What good looks like (domain-specific quality standards)
  5. Tools (domain-specific systems and integrations)
  6. Autonomy calibration (when to auto-execute vs. pause and ask)
  7. Sample scenarios (reference situations for this domain)

Output: Domain context summary

Approval Gate: None (informational read)

docs.title: Phase 4: Read Repo Context
docs.text:
Goal: Load repo-specific rules and pre-authorized operations.

  1. Read CLAUDE.md in the target repo (the repo where work will occur)
  2. Extract key sections:
  3. Required reading (what documentation applies)
  4. Non-negotiable rules (repo-specific constraints)
  5. Stack & Structure (tech choices, folder layout)
  6. Pre-authorized operations (what AI can do without asking)
  7. Always confirm before (what requires user approval)

Output: Repo context summary

Approval Gate: None (informational read)

docs.title: Phase 5: Validate and Assemble Context
docs.text:
Goal: Check for conflicts; assemble complete operating picture.

  1. Check for conflicts between context sources using this precedence:
  2. Hard Rules (GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md) override everything
  3. Repo rules (CLAUDE.md) can supplement but not contradict global rules
  4. Domain rules (domain context) are guidelines, not overrides
  5. If conflict detected, stop and report to user before proceeding
  6. Assemble summary: global + domain + repo context into single coherent picture

Output: Complete context summary with any flagged conflicts

Approval Gate: None (validation only)

docs.title: Phase 6: Return to Calling Skill
docs.text:
Goal: Pass context to downstream skill and resume execution.

  1. Present complete context summary to user (or proceed silently if already in-session and context is confirmed)
  2. State what assumptions have been made (if any)
  3. Return control to downstream skill with context established

Output: "Context established. [Domain]. [Assumptions, if any]. Ready to proceed with [downstream skill]."

Approval Gate: None (foundational setup complete)

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
Always deliver output in this structured format:

GLOBAL CONTEXT
- User: [Name]
- Primary roles: [roles]
- Communication style: [style traits]
- Hard Rules: [summary of 5 rules]

DOMAIN
- Domain: [domain name]
- Autonomy level: [advisory-only / execute-with-approval / auto-execute]
- Key tools: [tools specific to this domain]

REPO CONTEXT
- Repo: [repo name]
- Pre-authorized operations: [summary]
- Requires confirmation: [summary]

ASSUMPTIONS
- [Any judgment calls made with incomplete info]
- [Any ambiguous context clarifications]

STATUS: Context established. Ready to proceed.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Condition Resolution Context file missing or unreadable Stop. Report which file is missing. Ask user to verify file exists in repo root or domains/ folder. Domain is ambiguous or not stated Stop. Ask user to confirm which domain applies to this task. Do not guess. Conflict detected (repo rule contradicts global hard rule) Stop. Report specific conflict to user. Ask which rule takes precedence. Do not proceed until resolved. Repo-specific CLAUDE.md missing Proceed with global + domain context only. Note that repo-level rules are unavailable.

docs.title: Example 1: Professional Work in a Design System Repo
docs.text:
User Task: "Help me audit the design system tokens for compliance."

Execution:

  1. Read GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md → Identify: JB, direct/blunt communication, stable-over-speed preference
  2. Infer domain → "professional-work (design)" → confirmed
  3. Read domains/professional-work-context.md → Autonomy: "execute-with-approval for structural changes"
  4. Read design-system-repo/CLAUDE.md → Pre-auth: npm install, read-only operations
  5. Assemble context → Ready to run skill-audit-trigger (design system variant)

Output:

GLOBAL CONTEXT
- User: JB
- Roles: business ventures, professional work, parenting
- Style: Direct, blunt, concise
- Hard Rules: Credential protection, no direct main commits, no irreversible actions without confirmation

DOMAIN
- Domain: professional-work (design)
- Autonomy: Execute design audits; pause before structural changes
- Tools: GitHub, design-system repo, Tailwind, shadcn/ui

REPO CONTEXT
- Repo: design-system-shadcn-tailwind (GitHub)
- Pre-auth: Read files, grep, npm install, npx shadcn add
- Confirm before: git commit/push, deleting files, new npm packages

ASSUMPTIONS
- None

STATUS: Context established. Professional-work domain. Ready to proceed with skill-audit-trigger.

docs.title: Example 2: Business Venture with Ambiguous Domain
docs.text:
User Task: "I need to set up a financial tracking system for a side project."

Execution:

  1. Read GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md
  2. Infer domain → Could be "business-ventures" or "personal-processes" → Ambiguous, ask user

Output:

GLOBAL CONTEXT LOADED
- User: JB
- Communication: Direct, blunt, concise

DOMAIN AMBIGUOUS
This task could apply to:
a) business-ventures — if this is a business or revenue project
b) personal-processes — if this is a personal org system

Which domain applies to this project?

User confirms: "It's a business venture side project."

Execution continues: Read business-ventures domain context, read CLAUDE.md for target repo, assemble complete context.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
• Related skills: All downstream execution skills depend on this skill
  • External references:
  • GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md (global operating principles)
  • domains/[domain]-context.md (domain-specific rules)
  • CLAUDE.md (repo-level rules)
  • Decision Framework: See GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md section "Decision Framework"

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text:
• How often to review: Quarterly, or when GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md changes
  • What triggers an update: Changes to hard rules, additions of new domains, changes to context file structure
  • Owner: Global systems (applies to all domains)

docs.title: Notes for Skill Developers
docs.text:
Why this skill is foundational: - Context precedes execution. Without confirmed context, downstream skills will make wrong assumptions. - This skill is not optional. Every downstream skill calls this first, either explicitly or implicitly. - If a downstream skill encounters ambiguous context, it should pause and invoke this skill again.

How downstream skills use this: - Downstream skills inherit the context established here. - If context changes (e.g., user switches repos or domains), downstream skills should invoke this skill again. - Downstream skills trust the context returned by this skill; they do not re-read context files.

Extending this skill: - If new context sources are added (e.g., project-specific context), add a new phase here. - If context file locations change, update Phase 1–4 file paths. - Do not remove or reorder phases; downstream skills depend on this exact sequence.

docs.title: Skill: Research Synthesis
docs.text: Compile, organize, and synthesize research findings. Gathers sources, extracts key information, organizes by theme, synthesizes insights for academic work and analysis.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Attribute Value Skill ID skill-research-synthesis Type execution (procedural workflow) Version 1.0 Status ACTIVE Created 2026-04-08 Last Reviewed 2026-04-08 Dependencies skill-prompting-core Domains academic, business-advisory, finance-advisory

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Compile and synthesize research findings.

Researches a topic, locates relevant sources, extracts key information, organizes findings by theme, and synthesizes insights into coherent summaries. Supports academic work, business analysis, and decision-making research.

docs.title: When to Use This Skill
docs.text:
• Use this skill when: You need to research a topic and compile findings
  • Do NOT use this skill when: You need primary research (interviews, surveys) or original analysis
  • Prerequisite state: Clear research question or topic to investigate

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
• Research question or topic: What do you need to research?
  • Purpose: What will you use this research for? (assignment, decision-making, background, etc.)

docs.title: Optional Inputs
docs.text:
• Source preferences: Academic journals, news, books, websites? [TBD — specify if important]
  • Time frame: How recent should sources be?
  • Scope: Broad overview or deep dive on specific aspect?
  • Format preference: How do you want findings organized?

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
• You'll review and synthesize findings with your own judgment
  • I'm finding existing sources, not conducting original research
  • Citations and attribution will be proper throughout

docs.title: Phase 1: Define Research Scope
docs.text:
Goal: Clarify what you're researching and why.

  1. You provide: research question + context
  2. I clarify:
  3. "What specifically are you trying to understand?"
  4. "What will you do with these findings?"
  5. "Any constraints on sources or timeframe?"
  6. Confirm scope: "I'll research [topic] focusing on [angle]. Correct?"

Output: Clear research scope

Approval Gate: None (clarification only)

docs.title: Phase 2: Locate & Gather Sources
docs.text:
Goal: Find relevant sources.

  1. Search for sources:
  2. Academic databases (if available: JSTOR, Google Scholar, etc.)
  3. News and publications
  4. Books and reference materials

  5. Primary sources if applicable

  6. Evaluate sources:

  7. Relevance: Does it address your research question?
  8. Credibility: Is the source authoritative?
  9. Recency: How current is the information?

  10. Bias: Any apparent bias to note?

  11. Compile bibliography:

  12. Full citations with proper formatting
  13. Summary of each source's relevance
  14. Access information (online, library, etc.)

Output: Annotated bibliography

Approval Gate: None (informational)

docs.title: Phase 3: Extract Key Information
docs.text:
Goal: Identify and organize key findings.

  1. Read and extract:
  2. Main arguments or findings
  3. Supporting evidence and data
  4. Relevant quotes or examples

  5. Author's conclusions

  6. Organize by theme:

  7. Group findings around central themes
  8. Identify patterns and disagreements
  9. Note which sources support which arguments

  10. Track evidence strength (data, research, expert opinion, assertion)

  11. Create extraction summary:

  12. Key findings organized by theme
  13. Supporting sources for each finding
  14. Notable disagreements or debates
  15. Gaps in the research

Output: Thematic organization of findings

Approval Gate: None (foundational work)

docs.title: Phase 4: Synthesize Insights
docs.text:
Goal: Create coherent summary of what research shows.

  1. Identify patterns:
  2. What do sources agree on?
  3. Where do they disagree?
  4. What's the dominant view vs. minority views?

  5. What areas lack good research?

  6. Synthesize narrative:

  7. What does the research collectively show?
  8. How do different findings relate to each other?
  9. What are the key takeaways?

  10. What questions remain unanswered?

  11. Create synthesis document:

  12. Overview of research landscape
  13. Key findings organized thematically
  14. Areas of agreement and debate
  15. Gaps and questions for further research
  16. Full bibliography with citations

Output: Research synthesis document

Approval Gate: SOFT (review: "Does this capture what you needed?")

docs.title: Phase 5: Format for Your Use
docs.text:
Goal: Present findings in format you'll use.

  1. Format options:
  2. Study guide format (themes with supporting evidence)
  3. Literature review format (sources organized by argument)
  4. Briefing document format (executive summary + detail)

  5. Outline format (structured outline with citations)

  6. Tailor to purpose:

  7. For academic work: proper citations, formal structure
  8. For decision-making: findings with implications for decisions
  9. For background: clear overview of landscape

Output: Formatted research synthesis ready to use

Approval Gate: None (delivery complete)

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
Research Synthesis Document:

RESEARCH SYNTHESIS — [Topic]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RESEARCH QUESTION
[What you researched and why]

OVERVIEW
[Big-picture summary of what research shows]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

KEY FINDINGS BY THEME

Theme 1: [Main theme]
├─ Finding A: [What research shows]
│  └─ Sources: [Citation 1, Citation 2]
├─ Finding B: [What research shows]
│  └─ Sources: [Citation]
└─ Consensus: [Agreement or debate on this theme]

Theme 2: [Main theme]
├─ Finding A: [What research shows]
│  └─ Sources: [Citations]
└─ Consensus: [Agreement or debate]

[Continue for all major themes]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

AREAS OF AGREEMENT
[What sources agree on]

AREAS OF DEBATE
├─ Position A: [What some argue]
│  └─ Support: [Which sources, why]
├─ Position B: [Alternative argument]
│  └─ Support: [Which sources, why]
└─ Status: [Which view is dominant / still debated]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RESEARCH GAPS
[What's not well-researched or unclear]

IMPLICATIONS
[What this research means for your purpose]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

FULL BIBLIOGRAPHY

[Alphabetical list with full citations]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

docs.title: Style Rules
docs.text:
• Proper attribution: Every claim sourced back to literature
  • Balanced representation: Present multiple viewpoints fairly
  • Evidence hierarchy: Distinguish data/research from opinion
  • Clarity: Make complex concepts accessible
  • Completeness: Include citations for all key findings
  • Proper formatting: Citations consistent throughout [TBD — confirm with program]

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Condition Resolution Limited sources available Note the limitation. Include what's available. Flag research gaps. Sources disagree significantly Present all positions. Note which is dominant. Explain disagreement. Very recent topic with little research Gather what exists. Note that field is new. Include expert commentary if available. Technical or specialized topic Explain jargon clearly. Ensure synthesis is accessible. Too much information found Prioritize: most relevant, most recent, most authoritative. Organize into main findings + supplementary.

docs.title: Skill: Study Guide Creation
docs.text: Create effective study materials from course content. Distills complex material into clear, organized study guides that enable active learning and retention.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Attribute Value Skill ID skill-study-guide-creation Type execution (procedural workflow) Version 1.0 Status ACTIVE Created 2026-04-08 Last Reviewed 2026-04-08 Dependencies skill-prompting-core Domains parenting, academic

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Create study guides for active learning.

Analyzes course material, identifies key concepts, creates study guides with definitions, examples, practice problems, and self-testing elements. Designed for understanding and retention, not just memorization.

docs.title: When to Use This Skill
docs.text:
• Use this skill when: Need to create study materials for a test or unit of learning
  • Do NOT use this skill when: Student should create their own study materials (part of learning)
  • Prerequisite state: Course material or textbook available; learning objectives clear

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
• Course or topic: What's being studied?
  • Learning scope: Which chapters, units, or concepts?

docs.title: Optional Inputs
docs.text:
• Test format: Multiple choice, essays, problem-solving? [TBD — specify if important]
  • Learning level: Grade or age
  • Learning style preferences: Visual, hands-on, reading, discussion?
  • Test date: When is the test? (to plan study timeline)

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
• Study guide supplements learning, doesn't replace it
  • Learner will actively use materials (not passively read them)
  • Goal is understanding, not just test performance

docs.title: Phase 1: Analyze Course Material
docs.text:
Goal: Identify key concepts and learning objectives.

  1. Review course materials:
  2. Textbook chapters or readings
  3. Lecture notes or recordings
  4. Assignment requirements

  5. Test expectations or rubric (if available)

  6. Identify key concepts:

  7. Core ideas that everything else builds on
  8. Supporting concepts that explain core ideas
  9. Details and examples

  10. Applications or connections

  11. Assess scope:

  12. What's most important?
  13. What's secondary?
  14. What's nice-to-know?
  15. What's likely to be tested?

Output: Concept map and priority analysis

Approval Gate: None (analysis)

docs.title: Phase 2: Organize Concepts
docs.text:
Goal: Structure material for understanding.

  1. Create hierarchy:
  2. Main concept
  3. Supporting concepts
  4. Details and examples

  5. Connections to other topics

  6. Identify relationships:

  7. How do concepts relate?
  8. What causes what?
  9. What's similar or different?

  10. How do they apply together?

  11. Plan organization:

  12. Sequential: build from simple to complex
  13. Thematic: organize by topic
  14. Comparative: show how concepts compare
  15. Practical: organize by application

Output: Organized concept structure

Approval Gate: None (organization planning)

docs.title: Phase 3: Create Study Materials
docs.text:
Goal: Build materials for active learning.

  1. Create concept definitions:
  2. Clear, concise definitions
  3. Everyday language explanation
  4. Related concepts

  5. Common misconceptions

  6. Add examples:

  7. Real-world examples
  8. Textbook examples
  9. Edge cases

  10. Non-examples (what this isn't)

  11. Create practice problems:

  12. Simple (basic understanding)
  13. Medium (application)
  14. Complex (deep application)

  15. Worked solutions for each

  16. Add self-testing:

  17. Fill-in-the-blank sections
  18. Flashcard format
  19. Practice quizzes
  20. Self-check questions

Output: Complete study guide materials

Approval Gate: SOFT (review: "Does this cover the test material?")

docs.title: Phase 4: Organize for Use
docs.text:
Goal: Format study guide for easy access and active learning.

  1. Create clear structure:
  2. Table of contents
  3. Clear section headings
  4. Easy to navigate

  5. Visual hierarchy

  6. Design for active engagement:

  7. Space for student notes
  8. Self-test questions
  9. Fill-in sections

  10. Reflection prompts

  11. Add reference sections:

  12. Key formula sheet
  13. Timeline or chronology
  14. Comparison table
  15. Quick reference guide

Output: Formatted, organized study guide ready to use

Approval Gate: None (final product)

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
Study Guide Template:

STUDY GUIDE — [Topic/Chapter]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

KEY CONCEPTS

[Concept 1: Definition]
├─ Why it matters: [Connection to learning goals]
├─ Examples: [Real examples]
└─ Common misconceptions: [What students often get wrong]

[Concept 2: Definition]
├─ Why it matters: [Connection]
├─ How it connects to [other concept]: [Relationship]
└─ Examples: [Examples]

[Continue for each key concept...]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

HOW IT ALL FITS TOGETHER

[Concept map or narrative description of how concepts relate]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PRACTICE PROBLEMS

Easy (Just checking understanding):
├─ Problem 1: [Simple application]
└─ Problem 2: [Simple application]

Medium (Applying the concept):
├─ Problem 1: [Real application]
└─ Problem 2: [Real application]

Hard (Deep understanding):
├─ Problem 1: [Complex application]
└─ Problem 2: [Complex application]

[WORKED SOLUTIONS SECTION]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

SELF-CHECK

Can you explain:
├─ ☐ [Concept 1] and why it matters
├─ ☐ [Concept 2] and how it connects
└─ ☐ [How to apply it] in a new situation

Can you solve:
├─ ☐ [Type of problem] without notes
└─ ☐ [Harder type] with partial help

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

QUICK REFERENCE

[Key formulas, dates, definitions on one page]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

docs.title: Study Material Types
docs.text:
• Concept summaries: Clear definitions + examples
  • Comparison tables: How concepts differ
  • Timelines: Chronological overview
  • Process maps: Step-by-step processes
  • Diagram/visuals: Visual representation of concepts
  • Flashcards: For memorization
  • Practice quizzes: Self-testing
  • Worked examples: Model solutions

docs.title: Style Rules
docs.text:
• Clear and concise: Easy to understand, not wordy
  • Active learning: Questions and blanks, not passive reading
  • Examples first: Concept → example → definition (not definition → example)
  • Organized: Clear hierarchy and flow
  • Visual: Use formatting, structure, whitespace
  • Testable: Materials prepare for actual test format

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Condition Resolution Too much material Prioritize. Include must-know vs. nice-to-know. Create two levels. Learner still confused Add more examples. Simplify definitions. Use diagrams. Break into smaller concepts. Practice problems too hard Create easier versions first. Add worked examples. Build up difficulty. Not aligned to test Review test format/expectations. Adjust materials to match. Add test-style practice.

docs.title: Skill: Tutoring Framework
docs.text: Educational support and learning scaffolding. Explains concepts, creates study materials, guides assignments, builds learning supports.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Attribute Value Skill ID skill-tutoring-framework Type execution (procedural workflow) Version 1.0 Status ACTIVE Created 2026-04-08 Last Reviewed 2026-04-08 Dependencies skill-prompting-core Domains parenting

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Provide educational support and structured tutoring.

Explains concepts at appropriate learning levels, creates study guides and practice materials, guides assignment work without doing it, scaffolds complex topics into manageable steps.

docs.title: When to Use This Skill
docs.text:
• Use this skill when: Child needs help understanding a concept, preparing for a test, or completing homework
  • Do NOT use this skill when: Child is supposed to work independently (encourage that instead)
  • Prerequisite state: You know what topic needs help; child is ready to engage

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
• Topic or concept: What needs to be explained or learned
  • Learning context: Is this homework help, test prep, or building understanding?

docs.title: Optional Inputs
docs.text:
• Learning level: Grade or age (to calibrate explanation complexity)
  • Learning style: Visual, hands-on, reading/writing, discussion, etc.
  • Current understanding: What does the learner already know?

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
• Goal is understanding, not grades
  • Learner is engaged and ready to work
  • You'll review materials and provide guidance, not just accept them as done
  • Academic integrity matters (teaching skill, not providing answers)

docs.title: Phase 1: Assess Current Understanding
docs.text:
Goal: Know where the learner is starting from.

  1. Ask clarifying questions:
  2. "What do you already know about [topic]?"
  3. "What part is confusing?"

  4. "What have you tried so far?"

  5. Identify gaps:

  6. What foundations are solid?
  7. What's missing or misunderstood?

  8. What's the key concept that's stuck?

  9. Assess learning preferences:

  10. Does the learner prefer examples, visuals, hands-on, discussion?
  11. How does this learner best absorb new information?

Output: Understanding of learner's current state and needs

Approval Gate: None (assessment)

docs.title: Phase 2: Explain Core Concept
docs.text:
Goal: Make the concept clear and understandable.

  1. Start simple:
  2. One-sentence summary
  3. Use everyday language

  4. Avoid jargon initially

  5. Build in layers:

  6. Core idea first
  7. Supporting concepts
  8. Details and nuances

  9. Advanced extensions

  10. Use multiple modalities:

  11. Examples (real-world or relatable)
  12. Analogies ("It's like...")
  13. Visuals (if helpful)

  14. Hands-on if applicable

  15. Check understanding:

  16. Ask: "Does that make sense?"
  17. Ask them to explain back: "How would you describe this?"
  18. Identify confusion and re-explain

Output: Clear explanation of concept

Approval Gate: SOFT (check: "Understand so far?")

docs.title: Phase 3: Practice & Reinforcement
docs.text:
Goal: Build fluency and confidence with the concept.

  1. Create practice opportunities:
  2. Simple examples first
  3. Gradually increase difficulty

  4. Varied problem types

  5. Guide practice (don't do it for them):

  6. "Try this one. What would you do first?"
  7. "You're stuck on [step]. Why do you think that's hard?"

  8. Guide to solution, don't provide answer

  9. Provide feedback:

  10. "You got [this part] right. Here's why [other part] needs adjustment."
  11. Reinforce correct thinking

  12. Correct misunderstandings

  13. Track progress:

  14. Is confidence building?
  15. Are mistakes decreasing?
  16. Is understanding deepening?

Output: Practice materials and guided work

Approval Gate: None (ongoing practice)

docs.title: Phase 4: Create Study Materials (If Needed)
docs.text:
Goal: Build reusable learning resources.

  1. Assess if materials are needed:
  2. Will learner need to review later?
  3. Is this a complex topic worth documenting?

  4. Does learner benefit from having reference materials?

  5. Create appropriate materials:

  6. Study guide: key concepts, definitions, examples
  7. Practice problems: with worked solutions
  8. Summary sheet: core ideas on one page
  9. Comparison chart: how different ideas relate

  10. Flashcards: for memorization if needed

  11. Design for active recall:

  12. Materials should prompt thinking, not just reading
  13. Include practice questions
  14. Leave blanks for learner to fill in
  15. Self-testing elements

Output: Study materials tailored to topic and learner

Approval Gate: None (materials are tools, not commitment)

docs.title: Phase 5: Assignment Support (Homework Help)
docs.text:
Goal: Guide homework without doing it.

  1. Clarify the assignment:
  2. "What's the assignment asking you to do?"
  3. "What do you already understand?"

  4. "Where are you stuck?"

  5. Guide without answering:

  6. Break into steps: "First you'd... Then you'd..."
  7. Ask leading questions: "What would happen if...?"
  8. Help find resources: "You could look here for..."

  9. Model the thinking: "I would approach this by..."

  10. Check work (not grade):

  11. "Is your answer reasonable given [constraint]?"
  12. "Did you address all parts of the question?"

  13. "Is your reasoning clear?"

  14. Never just provide answers:

  15. Assignment teaches something
  16. Struggle is part of learning
  17. Your job is scaffolding, not completion

Output: Guided homework completion

Approval Gate: None (ongoing process)

docs.title: Phase 6: Test Prep (If Applicable)
docs.text:
Goal: Prepare for assessment.

  1. Review key concepts:
  2. "What topics are on the test?"
  3. Create review materials covering those topics

  4. Revisit difficult concepts

  5. Practice test conditions:

  6. Create practice test or problems
  7. Timed practice (if test is timed)

  8. Similar format to real test

  9. Identify weak spots:

  10. Track which topics are still unclear
  11. Focus practice on those areas

  12. Re-explain if needed

  13. Build confidence:

  14. Show improvement over time
  15. Positive framing: "You know this now"
  16. Set realistic expectations

Output: Test-prep materials and practice

Approval Gate: None (prep continues until test)

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text: Tutoring materials vary by type, but always include:

docs.title: For Concept Explanation:
docs.text:
CONCEPT: [Name]

Simple explanation:
[One clear sentence explanation]

Deeper explanation:
[Expanded explanation with structure]

Examples:
├─ Example 1: [Real or relatable example]
├─ Example 2: [Another angle]
└─ Non-example: [What this is NOT]

How it works:
[Step-by-step process or mechanism]

Common misconceptions:
├─ Misconception 1: [What students often think]
│  └─ Correction: [Why that's wrong]
└─ Misconception 2: [...]

docs.title: For Study Guides:
docs.text:
STUDY GUIDE: [Topic]

Key concepts:
├─ Concept 1: [Definition]
├─ Concept 2: [Definition]
└─ Concept 3: [Definition]

How they relate:
[Diagram or description of relationships]

Practice problems:
├─ Easy: [Problem 1, Problem 2]
├─ Medium: [Problem 3, Problem 4]
└─ Hard: [Problem 5, Problem 6]

[Worked solutions for each]

Common errors to avoid:
├─ Error 1: [Description]
│  └─ Avoid by: [What to do instead]
└─ Error 2: [...]

docs.title: For Homework Guidance:
docs.text:
ASSIGNMENT: [What it is]

What the assignment is asking:
[Clarification of assignment]

How to approach it:
Step 1: [What you'd do first]
Step 2: [What next]
Step 3: [Then]
[...]

Resources you need:
├─ Resource A: [Where to find it]
└─ Resource B: [Where to find it]

Common mistakes to watch for:
├─ Mistake 1: [What students often get wrong]
└─ Mistake 2: [...]

Checking your work:
├─ Question 1: [Self-check]
├─ Question 2: [Self-check]
└─ Question 3: [Self-check]

docs.title: Style Rules
docs.text:
• Age-appropriate language: Explain at the learner's level, not adult level
  • Active over passive: Teach the skill, not just the answer
  • Encourage struggle: Some confusion is healthy learning
  • Celebrate growth: Notice improvement and effort
  • Academic integrity: Never write assignments for learner
  • Clear standards: Explain what "good" looks like
  • Progress tracking: Help learner see improvement over time

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Condition Resolution Learner doesn't understand explanation Try a different approach: visuals, analogies, real-world examples, hands-on Learner doesn't want to engage Pause. Find out what's blocking (frustration, tired, doesn't see value). Address root cause. Assignment is too hard Break into smaller pieces. Simplify first step. Build confidence before tackling complexity. Learner wants you to do the work Clarify: you teach, they do. Explain why that matters. Keep assignments their responsibility. Concept still not making sense Go back to basics. Check if foundational skill is missing. Don't move forward until foundation is solid.

docs.title: Example 1: Explaining Fractions to Elementary Learner
docs.text:
Simple explanation: "A fraction is a part of a whole. Like if you cut a pizza into 4 pieces and eat 1, you ate 1/4 of the pizza."

Build layers: - Numerator (top) = how many parts - Denominator (bottom) = how many parts the whole is divided into - 3/4 means 3 pieces when the pizza is cut into 4 pieces

Examples: - 1/2 = half (cut in 2 pieces, take 1) - 2/3 = two-thirds (cut in 3 pieces, take 2) - 5/5 = the whole thing (all the pieces)

Check: "If I cut a pie into 3 pieces and you take 2, what fraction did you take?" (Answer: 2/3)

docs.title: Example 2: Study Guide for History Topic
docs.text:
STUDY GUIDE: The American Revolution

Key dates:
├─ 1775: First battles (Lexington, Concord)
├─ 1776: Declaration of Independence
└─ 1783: Treaty of Paris (war ends)

Key people:
├─ George Washington: Led the Continental Army
├─ Benjamin Franklin: Diplomat, got French support
└─ Thomas Jefferson: Wrote Declaration

Why it happened:
1. Britain taxed colonies heavily (without representation)
2. Colonists felt unfair treatment
3. Wanted self-government and independence

What changed:
- Before: Colonies ruled by British government
- After: Independent United States

Practice:
- Q: What did colonists mean by "no taxation without representation"?
  A: They wanted to vote on taxes, not have them decided by Britain

- Q: How did France help the colonists?
  A: Provided military support, weapons, loans, and French soldiers

What to remember for test:
- Year of Declaration: 1776
- Main cause: taxation and lack of representation
- Result: Independent United States

docs.title: Example 3: Homework Guidance
docs.text:
Assignment: Write a paragraph explaining photosynthesis

What it's asking: Explain in your own words how plants make food

How to approach: 1. Write what happens: "Plants take sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide..." 2. Explain why: "This provides energy for the plant to grow" 3. Connect to learning: "This is why plants need sunlight and water"

Resources: - Your textbook section 3.2 - Khan Academy video on photosynthesis

Check your work: - Does my paragraph explain what photosynthesis is? - Did I mention sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide? - Did I explain what the plant gets from it?

docs.title: Skill: Volunteer Operations
docs.text: Manage volunteer organization operations. Handles scheduling, documentation, training, coordination, and operational systems for volunteer-led organizations.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Attribute Value Skill ID skill-volunteer-operations Type execution (procedural workflow) Version 1.0 Status ACTIVE Created 2026-04-08 Last Reviewed 2026-04-08 Dependencies skill-prompting-core Domains volunteer

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Build and maintain volunteer organization operations.

Creates scheduling systems, operational documentation, training materials, volunteer tracking, and coordination processes. Enables smooth operation of volunteer-led initiatives.

docs.title: When to Use This Skill
docs.text:
• Use this skill when: Setting up or improving volunteer organization operations
  • Do NOT use this skill when: Making decisions about organization strategy or time commitments
  • Prerequisite state: You've decided to run a volunteer organization; need operational systems

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
• Organization or program name: What are you organizing?
  • Operational area: Scheduling, documentation, training, tracking, coordination?

docs.title: Optional Inputs
docs.text:
• Current process: How is this being done now (if at all)?
  • Constraints: Budget, tools available, volunteer availability?
  • Scale: How many volunteers, how often do they meet?

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
• Operational decisions are yours; I'm building systems
  • You have basic tools available (spreadsheets, docs, calendar)
  • Volunteers are reliable enough for automated systems

docs.title: Phase 1: Assess Operational Need
docs.text:
Goal: Understand what operational system is needed.

  1. You provide: organization + operational need
  2. I assess:
  3. What currently exists?
  4. What's breaking or missing?
  5. What's the scale/complexity?
  6. Confirm scope: "I'll build [system] for [purpose]. Correct?"

Output: Clear operational scope

Approval Gate: None (clarification)

docs.title: Phase 2: Design System
docs.text:
Goal: Plan the operational system before building.

  1. Assess requirements:
  2. Who uses it? (volunteers, coordinators, you)
  3. What data/information is needed?
  4. What decisions does it inform?

  5. How frequently is it used?

  6. Design structure:

  7. Data model: what information is tracked?
  8. Workflow: how does it work step-by-step?
  9. Roles: who does what?

  10. Tools: spreadsheet, doc, calendar, other?

  11. Plan for maintenance:

  12. How will you keep it updated?
  13. Who's responsible for maintenance?
  14. How often does it need review?

Output: System design

Approval Gate: SOFT (review design: "Does this work for you?")

docs.title: Phase 3: Build System Components
docs.text:
Goal: Create the actual operational systems.

  1. Build components:
  2. Scheduling: volunteer availability and assignments
  3. Tracking: volunteer hours, tasks, attendance
  4. Documentation: procedures, checklists, guidelines
  5. Training: materials for new volunteers

  6. Communication: templates, coordination tools

  7. Create templates:

  8. Forms and checklists
  9. Communication templates
  10. Tracking sheets

  11. Documentation guides

  12. Set up structure:

  13. Folder organization
  14. File naming conventions
  15. Access and permissions
  16. Update processes

Output: Operational systems ready to use

Approval Gate: SOFT (test systems: "Are they usable?")

docs.title: Phase 4: Create Operations Guide
docs.text:
Goal: Document how the systems work.

  1. Write operations guide:
  2. How to use each system
  3. Who's responsible for what
  4. How to handle common scenarios

  5. Troubleshooting guide

  6. Create checklists:

  7. New volunteer onboarding
  8. Weekly/monthly routine
  9. Emergency procedures

  10. Review and maintenance

  11. Document processes:

  12. Volunteer communication
  13. Schedule management
  14. Conflict resolution
  15. Problem escalation

Output: Operations guide and checklists

Approval Gate: None (documentation)

docs.title: Phase 5: Train and Launch
docs.text:
Goal: Get systems into active use.

  1. Test with volunteers:
  2. Do volunteers understand the systems?
  3. What's confusing or broken?

  4. What needs adjustment?

  5. Refine based on feedback:

  6. Simplify confusing parts
  7. Add missing elements

  8. Adjust based on reality

  9. Launch operationally:

  10. Move to live use
  11. Monitor for issues
  12. Be ready to support

Output: Live operational systems

Approval Gate: None (systems operational)

docs.title: Scheduling System
docs.text:
• Volunteer availability tracking
  • Task/shift assignments
  • Calendar view of who's doing what
  • Conflict detection

docs.title: Volunteer Tracking

• Volunteer hours logged
  • Tasks completed tracked
  • Attendance records
  • Performance/reliability notes

docs.title: Communication System

  • Escalation procedures
  • Announcement distribution
  • Feedback collection

docs.title: Documentation System
docs.text:
• Organization procedures
  • Role descriptions
  • Checklists for tasks
  • Emergency contacts and protocols

docs.title: Training System
docs.text:
• New volunteer orientation
  • Role-specific training
  • Skills certification (if applicable)
  • Ongoing education

docs.title: Style Rules
docs.text:
• Keep it simple: Systems should be easy to understand and use
  • Document everything: Procedures should be clear
  • Automate where possible: Reduce manual work
  • Plan for handoff: Systems should work even if you're not there
  • Regular review: Build in quarterly check-ins

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Condition Resolution Volunteer doesn't show up Have backup plan. Log absence. Follow up to understand. System isn't being used Make it simpler or more essential. Get buy-in from volunteers. Volunteer misunderstands role Improve documentation. Add training. Be explicit about expectations. Communication breaking down Use multiple channels. Confirm receipt. Send reminders. Scaling issues Add more volunteers to coordination. Split responsibilities. Create deputy roles.

docs.title: Skill: Autonomous Project Build
docs.text: Execute a complete project from specification through autonomous operation. Chains kickoff assessment, domain context loading, skill orchestration, execution, monitoring, and feedback loops into a single end-to-end workflow.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Attribute Value Skill ID skill-autonomous-project-build Type orchestration (skill chaining) Version 1.0 Status ACTIVE Created 2026-04-08 Last Reviewed 2026-04-08 Dependencies skill-prompting-core, skill-project-kickoff, skill-audit-trigger, domain-specific execution skills Domains All (domain-agnostic chaining)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Build and execute a complete project autonomously.

Takes a validated project from kickoff specification through full execution, monitoring, and autonomous refinement. Orchestrates domain context, selects appropriate execution skills, handles execution phases, monitors for issues, and enables autonomous feedback loops.

docs.title: When to Use This Skill
docs.text:
• Use this skill when: You have a project specification ready for execution and want autonomous operation
  • Do NOT use this skill when: Project still needs clarification (use skill-project-kickoff first)
  • Prerequisite state: Validated project specification with approved SPEC and execution readiness confirmed

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
• Project Specification: Complete SPEC from skill-project-kickoff output (context, assessment, landscape, decision, specifications, readiness)
  • Approval: Human confirmation to proceed with autonomous execution
  • Success Criteria: What indicates the project is complete or successful?

docs.title: Optional Inputs
docs.text:
• Monitoring Dashboard: Metrics/checkpoints to track during execution
  • Feedback Frequency: How often should autonomous system check for feedback?
  • Escalation Rules: Conditions that trigger human intervention

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
• Project specification is complete and validated
  • Necessary skills exist and are documented in SKILL-REGISTRY.md
  • Domain context is available in domains/ folder
  • Execution is possible within AI operating constraints (not prohibited actions)

docs.title: Phase 1: Validate Specification & Load Context
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure project is ready for autonomous execution.

  1. Validate specification:
  2. SPEC is complete (objectives, constraints, success criteria)
  3. Readiness assessment passed
  4. All TBDs resolved or explicitly accepted

  5. No prohibited actions in project plan

  6. Load context (skill-prompting-core):

  7. Read GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md
  8. Infer domain
  9. Read domain context file

  10. Validate autonomy level allows execution

  11. Confirm execution readiness:

  12. "This project [name] is ready for autonomous execution. Proceed?"
  13. Human approval required before continuing

Output: Validated project context and autonomy confirmation

Approval Gate: HARD (human confirms execution)

docs.title: Phase 2: Select & Configure Execution Skills
docs.text:
Goal: Map project to appropriate execution skills.

  1. Analyze project requirements:
  2. What type of work? (execution vs. advisory)
  3. Which domain? (maps to domain skills)

  4. Which phase(s)? (identify skill sequence)

  5. Select skills:

  6. Primary skill: which execution skill handles main work?
  7. Supporting skills: which skills support/complement?
  8. Data flow: what's output of one, input to next?

  9. Error handling: what if primary skill encounters issues?

  10. Configure execution:

  11. Skill order (sequence and dependencies)
  12. Input mapping (what goes into each skill)
  13. Output capture (what to store from each skill)
  14. Integration points (where humans review/decide)

Output: Execution plan with skill sequence and configuration

Approval Gate: SOFT (review: "Skill sequence looks right?")

docs.title: Phase 3: Execute Primary Workflow
docs.text:
Goal: Run the main project execution.

  1. Execute skill sequence:
  2. Run first skill with spec-derived inputs
  3. Capture outputs
  4. Feed to next skill
  5. Continue through sequence

  6. Monitor for errors/escalations

  7. Handle execution phases:

  8. Major phase complete: pause for review if configured
  9. Skill outputs expected data: continue
  10. Skill outputs unexpected results: escalate

  11. Error encountered: handle via error resolution (Phase 6)

  12. Track progress:

  13. What's been completed?
  14. What's in progress?
  15. What's pending?
  16. Are we tracking against success criteria?

Output: Execution complete with all skill outputs captured

Approval Gate: SOFT (major phase reviews if configured)

docs.title: Phase 4: Autonomous Monitoring & Feedback
docs.text:
Goal: Monitor execution and gather feedback without waiting for human intervention.

  1. Establish monitoring:
  2. Are success criteria being met?
  3. Are there early warnings of problems?
  4. Is execution tracking to plan?

  5. Do any results suggest refinement needed?

  6. Autonomous feedback loop:

  7. Document monitoring observations
  8. Identify patterns (working well, needs adjustment, blocked)
  9. Note successes and failures

  10. Capture what would help next iteration

  11. Escalation flags:

  12. Issues that require human decision
  13. Constraint violations discovered during execution
  14. Unclear results needing human judgment
  15. Opportunities for scope change

Output: Monitoring report with observations, patterns, escalations

Approval Gate: None (informational)

docs.title: Phase 5: Refinement & Iteration
docs.text:
Goal: Improve execution based on monitoring.

  1. Analyze monitoring data:
  2. What worked? What didn't?
  3. Why did things work/not work?
  4. What would improve next iteration?

  5. Are success criteria tracking correctly?

  6. Propose refinements:

  7. Skill parameter adjustments
  8. Input data modifications
  9. Workflow sequence changes

  10. Success criteria clarification

  11. Implement refinements:

  12. Apply adjustments autonomously where autonomy allows
  13. Flag decisions requiring human input
  14. Re-execute affected phases
  15. Compare results to pre-refinement

Output: Refined execution with improvements documented

Approval Gate: SOFT (review refinements if major changes)

docs.title: Phase 6: Error & Escalation Handling
docs.text:
Goal: Handle problems that arise during execution.

Triggers: Skill errors, constraint violations, unclear results, scope questions

  1. Error assessment:
  2. Is this a retry-able error? (bad input, transient issue)
  3. Is this a skill limitation? (skill can't handle this)
  4. Is this an autonomy issue? (hits constraint)

  5. Is this a human decision needed? (unclear next step)

  6. Resolution paths:

  7. Retry: Fix input, re-run skill
  8. Adapt: Choose different skill for this phase
  9. Escalate: Flag for human decision

  10. Refine: Adjust spec and re-start affected phase

  11. Document escalation:

  12. What triggered it?
  13. What was attempted?
  14. Why did it fail?
  15. What human decision needed?

Output: Error resolution or escalation report

Approval Gate: HARD (human decides resolution for escalations)

docs.title: Phase 7: Completion & Close-Out
docs.text:
Goal: Complete project, document results, enable autonomous continuation.

  1. Verify completion:
  2. Are all success criteria met?
  3. Are all deliverables complete?
  4. Are all outputs documented?

  5. Are there any remaining open issues?

  6. Document results:

  7. What was built?
  8. What was learned?
  9. What changed from original spec?

  10. What would improve next iteration?

  11. Enable continuation:

  12. Is this a one-time project or ongoing?
  13. If ongoing: what's the maintenance workflow?
  14. If one-time: what's the handoff/archive process?

  15. What monitoring should continue?

  16. Close-out:

  17. Final output package (all deliverables)
  18. Completion report (what was done)
  19. Lessons learned (what worked, what to improve)
  20. Next steps (if any)

Output: Project completion report and artifact package

Approval Gate: None (project complete)

docs.title: Orchestration Pattern
docs.text:
Phase 1: Validate & Load Context
  ↓
Phase 2: Select Skills
  ↓
Phase 3: Execute Primary Workflow ← Main Execution Loop
  ├─→ [Skill Sequence: Primary → Supporting Skills]
  ├─→ [Output Capture & Monitoring]
  └─→ [Error Handling ← Phase 6]
  ↓
Phase 4: Monitoring & Feedback
  ↓
Phase 5: Refinement & Iteration
  ├─→ [May loop back to Phase 3 for re-execution]
  └─→ [May loop to Phase 2 for skill re-selection]
  ↓
Phase 6: Error & Escalation Handling
  ├─→ [Retry: Phase 3]
  ├─→ [Adapt: Phase 2]
  └─→ [Escalate: Human decision required]
  ↓
Phase 7: Completion & Close-Out

docs.title: Example 1: Business Opportunity Assessment
docs.text:
Project: Evaluate new business opportunity
Spec: Market research needed, financial viability assessment, decision required

Skill Sequence:
1. skill-research-synthesis (gather market/competitive data)
   ↓
2. skill-business-assessment (evaluate opportunity)
   ↓
3. skill-financial-assessment (financial viability)
   ↓
Output: Assessment report with GO/CAUTION/PASS recommendation

docs.title: Example 2: Academic Project Execution
docs.text:
Project: Research paper from topic to draft
Spec: Topic defined, deadline clear, requirements understood

Skill Sequence:
1. skill-research-synthesis (gather sources)
   ↓
2. skill-academic-writing → Phase 1-2 (understand assignment, develop thesis)
   ↓
3. skill-study-guide-creation (optional: create reference materials)
   ↓
4. skill-academic-writing → Phase 3-5 (outline, write, polish)
   ↓
Output: Polished paper ready to submit

docs.title: Example 3: Volunteer Operation Setup
docs.text:
Project: Launch volunteer scheduling system
Spec: Number of volunteers, frequency, tasks defined

Skill Sequence:
1. skill-volunteer-operations → Phase 1-2 (assess, design)
   ↓
2. skill-volunteer-operations → Phase 3-4 (build, document)
   ↓
3. skill-volunteer-operations → Phase 5 (train, launch)
   ↓
Output: Live volunteer system with documentation

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
Autonomous Project Build Report:

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

EXECUTION SUMMARY

├─ [Skill 1: What it did]
├─ [Skill 2: What it did]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHASE RESULTS

DELIVERABLES PACKAGE

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Condition Resolution Specification incomplete Phase 1 rejects execution; return to skill-project-kickoff Autonomy level too low Phase 1 identifies; escalate for human decision on how to proceed Skill not found Phase 2 identifies missing skill; propose alternative skill or escalate Skill execution fails Phase 6 handles; retry with adjusted inputs or switch to alternative skill Success criteria unclear Phase 4 monitoring flags; refine criteria and re-check Unexpected results Phase 5 analyzes; propose refinement or escalate if unclear Skill conflict (outputs incompatible) Phase 3 during execution; Phase 6 escalates or adapts workflow Execution blocked (constraint) Phase 6 identifies; escalate or refine approach Infinite loop (skill keeps failing) Phase 6 detects after N retries; escalate for human decision

docs.title: Style Rules
docs.text:
• Project-centric: Everything in service of completing the project specification
  • Autonomous mindset: Make decisions within autonomy constraints; escalate when unsure
  • Monitoring aware: Track against success criteria, not just task completion
  • Feedback responsive: Use monitoring data to refine execution, not just report
  • Learning oriented: Document lessons for next iteration
  • Error transparent: Explain error resolution steps clearly
  • Constraint respect: Never exceed autonomy boundaries; escalate instead

docs.title: Skill: Project Kickoff
docs.text: End-to-end project initialization workflow. Chains context establishment → opportunity assessment → decision → specification → execution readiness → launch.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text: Attribute Value Skill ID skill-project-kickoff Type orchestration (workflow sequencing) Version 1.0 Status ACTIVE Created 2026-04-08 Last Reviewed 2026-04-08 Dependencies skill-prompting-core, skill-audit-trigger, [future: skill-solution-assessment, skill-doc-classifier] Domains All

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Initialize any new project from idea to execution readiness.

This orchestration skill sequences multiple skills to move a project from conception to execution-ready state. It ensures you don't build the wrong thing, understand the landscape, make deliberate decisions, and have clear specifications before starting.

docs.title: When to Use This Skill
docs.text:
• Use this skill when: Starting a new project, initiative, or major feature across any domain
  • Do NOT use this skill when: Continuing work on an existing project (use domain-specific execution skills instead)
  • Prerequisite state: You have an idea or direction you want to explore

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text:
• Project or idea: Description of what you're thinking about starting
  • Domain: Which domain(s) this applies to (inferred if unclear, confirmed before proceeding)

docs.title: Optional Inputs
docs.text:
• Success criteria: What would make this project successful? (if you have them)
  • Constraints: Timeline, budget, resource limits (if relevant)

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
• You're ready to commit time to the project (at least exploration phase)
  • Relevant domain context is current and available
  • You'll make final decisions at each gate, not delegate them

docs.title: Phase 1: Establish Context (skill-prompting-core)
docs.text:
Goal: Load operating principles for this project.

  1. Run skill-prompting-core
  2. Read GLOBAL-CONTEXT.md
  3. Read domain-context.md (infer domain from project description)
  4. Read repo/tool CLAUDE.md files if applicable
  5. Return complete context to project-kickoff

Output: Complete operating context (global + domain + repo)

Approval Gate: None (foundational setup)

docs.title: Phase 2: Assess Opportunity (skill-audit-trigger or solution-assessment)
docs.text:
Goal: Evaluate if this is worth doing before investing time.

Currently available: Use skill-audit-trigger for assessment Future: Upgrade to skill-solution-assessment when available

  1. Run assessment:
  2. Does this already exist as free/available solution?
  3. Is this aligned with your goals and priorities?
  4. Are there blockers or dependencies?

  5. What's the effort estimate?

  6. Report findings:

  7. CRITICAL issues that block the project
  8. Major risks or concerns
  9. Opportunities or advantages
  10. Recommendation: proceed / reconsider / redesign

Output: Assessment report with recommendation

Approval Gate: YES — "Should I proceed with this project?" - If NO: Project paused or abandoned. Done. - If YES: Continue to Phase 3 - If REDESIGN: Go back to Phase 1 with revised idea

docs.title: Phase 3: Understand Landscape (skill-audit-trigger or doc-classifier)
docs.text:
Goal: Research existing solutions, patterns, and examples.

Currently available: Use skill-audit-trigger for relevant docs/patterns Future: Upgrade to skill-doc-classifier when available

  1. Run landscape research:
  2. Find similar projects or patterns
  3. Identify existing solutions or approaches
  4. Gather relevant documentation

  5. Identify key patterns or best practices

  6. Synthesize findings:

  7. Here's what similar projects look like
  8. Common approaches and their trade-offs
  9. Relevant documentation and resources
  10. Recommended approach based on landscape

Output: Landscape analysis with recommended approach

Approval Gate: SOFT (informational) - Review landscape analysis - Decide: "Does this approach feel right? Any adjustments?" - You can iterate here without hard approval needed

docs.title: Phase 4: Make Decision & Define Specification
docs.text:
Goal: Create clear specification before starting work.

  1. Decision point:
  2. You confirm: "Yes, I want to build this using [approach]"

  3. Document decision: what are you building, why, success criteria

  4. Create specification:

  5. Project name and purpose
  6. Success criteria (what done looks like)
  7. Scope (what's in, what's out, what's phase 2)
  8. Approach and key decisions
  9. Timeline and resource estimate
  10. Dependencies or blockers
  11. Next steps

Output: Specification document (SPEC.md or similar)

Approval Gate: YES — "Is this SPEC clear and correct?" - If NO: Revise specification - If YES: Continue to Phase 5

docs.title: Phase 5: Execution Readiness
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure you're ready to start.

  1. Checklist:
  2. ✅ Context understood (domain, rules, autonomy levels)
  3. ✅ Decision made (proceed with confidence)
  4. ✅ Specification clear (what/why/success defined)
  5. ✅ Approach chosen (how you'll build)
  6. ✅ Resources available (time, tools, access)

  7. ✅ Blockers identified (and plan to address them)

  8. Identify starting skill:

  9. "Which skill should run first?"
  10. Examples: skill-block-intake (design system), skill-audit-trigger (audit), [domain-specific skill]

Output: Readiness checklist + identified starting skill

Approval Gate: SOFT (confirm readiness, not decision) - "Ready to execute?" → proceed

docs.title: Phase 6: Launch
docs.text:
Goal: Begin execution with clarity.

  1. Confirm starting skill and parameters
  2. Hand off to execution skill
  3. Track and report on execution

Output: Project launched into execution phase

Approval Gate: None (execution begins)

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
Project Kickoff Report:

PROJECT KICKOFF — [PROJECT NAME]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CONTEXT

Domain: [Domain]
Autonomy level: [For this domain]
Related docs: [Key docs to reference]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

OPPORTUNITY ASSESSMENT

Recommendation: [Proceed / Reconsider / Redesign]
Key findings:
├─ [Finding 1]
├─ [Finding 2]
└─ [Finding 3]

Critical issues: [If any, list them]
Effort estimate: [High / Medium / Low]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

LANDSCAPE ANALYSIS

Similar projects / patterns:
├─ [Pattern A] — [how it works]
├─ [Pattern B] — [how it works]
└─ [Recommended approach] — [why this approach]

Key resources:
├─ [Resource 1]
├─ [Resource 2]
└─ [Resource 3]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PROJECT SPECIFICATION

Name: [Project name]
Purpose: [1-2 sentence purpose]

Success Criteria:
├─ [Criterion 1 — how you'll measure success]
├─ [Criterion 2 — how you'll measure success]
└─ [Criterion 3 — how you'll measure success]

Scope:
├─ In: [What's included in this project]
├─ Out: [What's explicitly not included]
└─ Phase 2: [Future work, if applicable]

Approach:
├─ Overall approach: [Describe the approach]
├─ Key decisions: [Decision 1, decision 2]
└─ Technology / tools: [What you'll use]

Timeline: [Estimate: days / weeks / months]
Resources needed: [People, tools, budget, access]
Blockers / Dependencies: [List any blockers and mitigation]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

EXECUTION READINESS

Checklist:
├─ ✅ Context understood (domain, rules, autonomy)
├─ ✅ Decision made (proceed with confidence)
├─ ✅ Specification clear (what/why/success defined)
├─ ✅ Approach chosen (how you'll build)
├─ ✅ Resources available (time, tools, access)
└─ ✅ Blockers identified (and plan to address)

Starting skill: [skill-name]
First steps: [What executes first]

Status: READY TO LAUNCH

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

docs.title: Style Rules
docs.text:
• Clear decisions at each gate: Don't skip approval gates; each one informs the next
  • Specification is the contract: Once SPEC is approved, it drives execution
  • Blockers surface early: Identify constraints before starting
  • Domain autonomy respected: Decisions stay in appropriate autonomy level per domain
  • Connected to starting skill: Project kickoff hands off to a specific skill with clear parameters

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Condition Resolution Domain is ambiguous Pause Phase 1. Ask user to confirm which domain applies. Assessment recommends against proceeding Stop. Discuss redesign or abandonment. Don't force ahead. Specification is unclear Return to Phase 4. Iterate until SPEC is clear. Blockers appear blocking Address blockers before launching. Create separate project for blocker if needed. No clear starting skill Identify first skill together. Some projects need custom sequencing.

docs.title: Project: Design System Component
docs.text:
Phase 1: Context loaded (professional-work domain, design-system repo context)

Phase 2: Assessment → "Does similar component exist? Can we use shadcn/ui variant instead of building custom? Effort: Easy if shadcn, Moderate if custom."

Decision: Use shadcn variant + small custom extensions.

Phase 3: Landscape → "shadcn Alert component with custom styling. Similar patterns in [existing components]. Recommended approach: extend shadcn Alert with custom variant."

Phase 4: Specification → - Component: Alert with custom warning variant - Success: Storybook stories, TypeScript types, design-system integration - Approach: Extend shadcn + Tailwind tokens - Timeline: 2 hours - Starting skill: skill-block-intake (if importing from template) OR direct component implementation

Phase 5: Ready → All checks pass

Phase 6: Launch → skill-block-intake runs, or direct implementation begins

docs.title: Related Skills

docs.title: Notes for Skill Developers
docs.text:
Why orchestration? - Project kickoff sequences multiple skills in a specific order - Each phase depends on previous phases - Gates (approval points) ensure deliberate decision-making - This is the highest-level skill; most other skills are called by this one

Customization by domain: - Each domain has different autonomy levels and constraints - Phase 1 pulls domain context, which then shapes all subsequent phases - Examples: business-ventures requires "never financial commitment without approval," professional-work requires "execute-with-approval for approach"

When to upgrade: - When skill-solution-assessment is built, upgrade Phase 2 to use it - When skill-doc-classifier is built, upgrade Phase 3 to use it - These upgrades happen in SKILL-CHANGELOG as v1.1 (minor version bump, backward compatible)
